exemplar.title: How do the percentages of COVID-19 vaccinations (%) (from December 2020 to December 2022) in the middle-aged populations (from 24 to 48 years) in selected Middle Eastern countries(United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Bahrain, Oman, and Jordan) and Asian countries (Singapore, South Korea, Japan, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam) correlate with COVID-19 mortality (n per million) caused by the SARS-CoV-2 Omicron Variant?

detailedExemplarData.title: How do the percentages of COVID-19 vaccinations (%) (from December 2020 to December 2022) in the middle-aged populations (from 24 to 48 years) in selected Middle Eastern countries(United Arab Emirates, Qatar, Saudi Arabia, Kuwait, Bahrain, Oman, and Jordan) and Asian countries (Singapore, South Korea, Japan, Malaysia, Thailand, and Vietnam) correlate with COVID-19 mortality (n per million) caused by the SARS-CoV-2 Omicron Variant?

subject.title: Biology
subject.description:
**IB Biology** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **four interconnected themes: Unity and Diversity, Form and Function, Interaction and Interdependence, and Continuity and Change**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, scientific inquiry, data analysis, and practical investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice and data-based questions | 1 hour 20 min | 2 hours | 36% |
| **Paper 2** | Short and extended response questions | 1 hour 30 min | 2 hours 30 min | 44% |
| **Scientific Investigation** | Individual research project | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: The research question is framed within a highly specific epidemiological context (Omicron wave, age bracket 24â48ây, defined time windows, regional comparison).
Biological and public-health rationale is clearly articulated, situating the study in an appropriate scientific context.
Methodological considerations (data sources, inclusion/exclusion criteria, confounders) are described with justification, demonstrating thoughtful study design.
weaknesses: Minor ambiguity remains about the precise mortality metric and its calculation for the 13-day windows.
Some methodological descriptions lack quantitative thresholds (e.g., defining âsimilar healthcare accessâ), limiting full reproducibility.
Exact data-source URLs and median-age determination methods are not fully specified, leaving small gaps for replication.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The student meets the 3â4 band across methodological and context strands and edges towards 5 in research-question contextualisation. While a top-band score of 5â6 would require fully unambiguous, reproducible methodology with no omissions, the submissionâs minor ambiguities warrant a score of 4 for Research Design.

strengths: Data tables are clearly labelled with units and formulas, and a scatterplot with regression communicates the core patterns effectively.
Processing steps (rate formulas, Pearsonâs r, RÂ², IQR outlier method) are presented transparently, supporting basic traceability.
weaknesses: There is virtually no quantitative consideration of uncertainties (no error bars, confidence intervals or propagation of error).
Inconsistencies between raw counts and sample calculations, and a conceptual slip on null-hypothesis wording, indicate significant processing inaccuracies.
Presentation lacks precision in significant figures, axis labels, and full consistency in data normalization.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Despite clear communication of recording and basic processing, the near absence of uncertainty analysis and notable inconsistencies/inaccuracies restrict this criterion to the 1â2 band. Major omissions in uncertainty quantification and some analytical errors justify a score of 2.

strengths: The conclusion is directly relevant to the research question and consistently reflects the negative correlation found in the analysis.
Numerical outcomes (r, p-value) are cited, linking back to hypotheses and study objectives.
Findings are compared to accepted scientific context (WHO reports, systematic reviews), demonstrating a meaningful, though brief, contextualisation.
weaknesses: Some overgeneralisation suggests causation rather than correlation, preventing a fully justified (5â6) conclusion.
Notation inconsistencies (e.g., âÎ¼ per millionâ) may confuse readers.
Comparisons to literature are relevant but lack depth in discussing mechanisms or contrasting studies.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The conclusion meets the 3â4 band descriptors: it is relevant, generally consistent with analysis, and includes some scientific-context comparison. However, overstatements and brevity in contextual discussion cap the score at 3.

strengths: Specific methodological weaknesses (data transparency, exclusion bias, confounders) are identified and described in context.
Realistic improvements (excess-mortality cross-checks, qualitative surveys, multiple regression) are proposed and linked to identified limitations.
Critical reflection on sampling diversity and statistical validation shows engagement with study strengths and weaknesses.
weaknesses: The relative impact of each specific weakness is noted qualitatively but not quantitatively assessed.
Improvement proposals are described but not fully explained in terms of how they would be implemented or measured.
Some suggestions (e.g., survey design, regression covariates) remain at a conceptual level without detailed follow-through.
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Evaluation strands sit solidly in the 3â4 band: specific weaknesses and relevant improvements are described, but neither the relative impacts nor the mitigation steps are fully explained. This warrants a score of 3.

strandSelections.reasoning: The conclusion restates a statistically significant negative correlation, cites the numerical outcomes and links directly back to the research question. Despite the earlier wording confusion about null vs alternative, the final stance is broadly consistent with the presented analysis. Some over-generalisation (claiming causation) prevents a fully âjustifiedâ score, situating the work comfortably in the 3-4 band at 3.

Raw and processed data are organised into clearly labelled tables; units, rounding method and calculation equations are provided, and the scatterplot with regression line communicates patterns effectively. Minor imprecision (mixing absolute deaths and rates in early tables, inconsistent significant figures) limits full precision, but overall communication is clear OR precise, satisfying the 3-4 descriptor at the upper boundary.

Data processing includes correct formulas for rates and percentages, a Pearson r, p-value, RÂ² and linear model. Calculations appear internally consistent, though the narrative erroneously says the null hypothesis is *accepted* while describing rejection; such conceptual slip plus reliance on rounded figures constitute significant inaccuracies. Processing is therefore appropriate but with notable issues, aligning with the mid-band score of 3.

Specific weaknesses are identifiedâdata transparency, exclusion bias, unmeasured confoundersâand each is described in context of its possible effect (e.g., under-reporting deaths may inflate correlation). The impact is noted qualitatively but not quantitatively explored, satisfying the descriptor âdescribed specific weaknessesâ (3-4 band) at the lower edge.

The student compares findings with published literature citing WHO, systematic reviews and national data, arguing that the observed negative relationship aligns with accepted scientific understanding of vaccine efficacy. The comparison is relevant but brief and lacks depth on mechanisms or contrasting studies, matching the âsome relevant comparisonâ level (3-4 band).

The student frames a very specific epidemiological research question, clearly identifying the Omicron wave, a defined age bracket (24-48 y), explicit time window (Dec 2020-Dec 2022 / Jan 8-21 2022) and two regional groupings of countries. The accompanying introduction gives adequate biological background and explains why vaccination is expected to influence mortality. This situates the question in a precise, appropriate context rather than merely a broad COVID discussion, fulfilling the top-band descriptor; a single minor ambiguity about the exact mortality metric does not materially detract from contextualisation.

The student explicitly states that uncertainties for vaccination % and mortality could not be obtained and offers no numerical error estimates, confidence intervals or error bars. Apart from acknowledging this absence, there is virtually no attempt to quantify or propagate uncertainty, representing only limited evidence and therefore the bottom gradable band (1-2) with the minimum mark.

The report proposes realistic, relevant improvementsâcross-checking excess mortality, adding qualitative surveys, employing multiple regression, studying booster coverageâeach linked to an earlier limitation. Explanations of *how* these would mitigate the issues are short, so the response is more than a list yet stops short of full explanation, meriting a 3 in the 3-4 band.

Methodological factorsâchoice of secondary databases, selection criteria (data availability, healthcare comparability, Omicron predominance, conflict exclusion) and several controlled variablesâare described with some justification. The student recognises confounders (healthcare capacity, age structure, prior waves) and notes absence of measurement uncertainties. While these considerations are relevant and mostly sufficient, explanations of how each factor might quantitatively influence data quality are brief; thus the work meets the descriptor for âdescribedâ rather than the fuller, causal âexplainedâ top level.

The data-collection procedure (country list, dates, variables, formulas for mortality per million and vaccination %) and statistical tests (Pearson, linear regression, IQR outlier check) are laid out so that another researcher could broadly replicate the study. However, population figures, raw vaccination counts and precise mortality sources are not fully tabulated, and the method of converting deaths to the 13-day window is unclear. These ambiguities prevent flawless reproducibility, so the strand sits solidly in the 3-4 band, nearer the lower end.

annotations.comment: Raw data mix of death counts and vaccination figures lacks normalization. Convert these to per-million rates prior to analysis to maintain consistency.
Table 6 appears empty in the OCR output. Verify that all vaccination data are correctly displayed or provide an appendix with the complete dataset.
The explicit formula for mortality rate calculation is clearly displayed, improving transparency of your data processing steps.
The sample mortality calculation uses 128 deaths, but your raw data table shows 23. Resolve this discrepancy to ensure consistency.
Clarify the denominator in the vaccination percentage formula (should read âtotal population of a countryâ) to correct the typographical error.

The binomial notation \(\binom{9}{0}\) in the research question appears extraneous and may confuse readers. Consider removing or correcting this notation to accurately express the vaccination timeframe.
The introduction clearly situates the epidemiological background of COVID-19 within a global health context, enhancing the relevance of the research question.
Add a specific citation for the statement on vaccine efficacy against Omicron to reinforce the claim with primary data and strengthen the biological rationale.
Figure 1 is not explicitly referenced in the methods narrative. Embed a description of how the SARS-CoV structure informs your study design to improve cohesion.
The null and alternative hypotheses are clearly stated and directly tied to the variables, supporting replicable statistical testing.
Provide exact source URLs and access dates for the John Hopkins and Our World in Data repositories to enable precise reproducibility of the data collection.
The acknowledgment of missing uncertainty data lacks any numerical estimate or discussion of how these gaps could influence the results. Consider at least a qualitative uncertainty assessment.
The selection criteria for countries are well-justified with clear inclusion and exclusion factors, ensuring comparability across regions.
Specify the precise method and data source used to determine each countryâs median age to ensure full transparency of demographic controls.
Control methods lack quantitative thresholds (e.g., defining âsimilar healthcare accessâ by specific metrics), which reduces reproducibility of the methodology.
Indicate whether assumptions for Pearsonâs correlation (normality, linearity) were tested and how you addressed any violations.
The ethical implications, including data transparency and bias concerns, are comprehensively addressed, demonstrating critical awareness of dataset limitations.
The âmiddle ageâ column is not used in subsequent analysis. Either integrate it into your models or remove it to keep the dataset focused.
Add axis labels, units, and the regression equation to the scatterplot to enhance interpretability.
The text incorrectly states acceptance of the null hypothesis when p < 0.05. Revise to state rejection of Hâ and acceptance of the alternative.
Include error bars or confidence bands around the regression line to visually represent uncertainty in the model fit.
The description of RÂ² as âthe more connected the dataâ is imprecise. Clarify that RÂ² represents the proportion of variance in mortality explained by vaccination.
Presentation of the IQR method and bound calculations is precise, supporting transparent outlier detection.
Quantify how much Jordanâs mortality rate deviates from the IQR bounds to strengthen the statistical outlier discussion.
Discuss potential confounders specific to the UAE (e.g., transient labor population) to enrich interpretation of its unexpectedly high mortality.
The strengths section effectively highlights diverse sampling and robust statistical validation, demonstrating critical reflection on methodology.
The limitation regarding qualitative factors could be expanded by outlining specific survey instruments or case-study designs.
The data transparency weakness is specific but could be strengthened by citing independent excess-mortality studies (e.g., WHO, CDC) for cross-validation.
Propose a concrete multiple-regression model (e.g., including ICU capacity and obesity prevalence) to address confounding variables quantitatively.
The conclusion introduces âÎ¼ per millionâ instead of ân per million,â which may confuse readers; standardize notation for consistency.
Strengthen the concluding paragraph by explicitly linking the multilevel strategy recommendation to the studyâs quantitative findings.
Bibliography entries include stray LaTeX commands (e.g., \overline{a}) and symbols. Ensure citations follow a consistent formatting style.

strands.name: Research question context
strands.content: The research question is outlined within a broad context.

strands.name: Methodological considerations
strands.content: Methodological considerations associated with collecting relevant and sufficient data to answer the research question are described.

strands.content: The description of the methodology for collecting or selecting data allows for the investigation to be reproduced with few ambiguities or omissions.

strands.name: Research question context
strands.content: The research question is described within a specific and appropriate context.

strands.name: Methodological considerations
strands.content: Methodological considerations associated with collecting relevant and sufficient data to answer the research question are explained.

strands.name: Methodology description
strands.content: The description of the methodology for collecting or selecting data allows for the investigation to be reproduced.

strands.name: Consideration of uncertainties
strands.content: The recording and processing of data shows limited evidence of the consideration of uncertainties.

strands.name: Data processing quality
strands.content: Some processing of data relevant to addressing the research question is carried out but with major omissions, inaccuracies or inconsistencies.

strands.name: Communication of data recording and processing
strands.content: The communication of the recording and processing of the data is either clear or precise.

strands.name: Consideration of uncertainties
strands.content: The recording and processing of data shows evidence of a consideration of uncertainties but with some significant omissions or inaccuracies.

strands.name: Data processing quality
strands.content: The processing of data relevant to addressing the research question is carried out but with some significant omissions, inaccuracies or inconsistencies.

strands.name: Communication of data recording and processing
strands.content: The communication of the recording and processing of the data is both clear and precise.

strands.name: Consideration of uncertainties
strands.content: The recording and processing of data shows evidence of an appropriate consideration of uncertainties.

strands.name: Data processing quality
strands.content: The processing of data relevant to addressing the research question is carried out appropriately and accurately.

strands.name: Conclusion relevance and consistency
strands.content: No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.

strands.name: Scientific context comparison
strands.content: No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.

strands.name: Conclusion relevance and consistency
strands.content: A conclusion is stated that is relevant to the research question but is not supported by the analysis presented.

strands.name: Scientific context comparison
strands.content: The conclusion makes superficial comparison to the accepted scientific context.

strands.name: Conclusion relevance and consistency
strands.content: A conclusion is described that is relevant to the research question but is not fully consistent with the analysis presented.

strands.name: Scientific context comparison
strands.content: A conclusion is described that makes some relevant comparison to the accepted scientific context.

strands.name: Conclusion relevance and consistency
strands.content: A conclusion is justified that is relevant to the research question and fully consistent with the analysis presented.

strands.name: Scientific context comparison
strands.content: A conclusion is justified through relevant comparison to the accepted scientific context.

strands.name: Methodological weaknesses
strands.content: No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.

strands.name: Improvements
strands.content: No work presented or work does not reach the standard described.

strands.name: Methodological weaknesses
strands.content: The report states generic methodological weaknesses or limitations.

strands.name: Improvements
strands.content: Realistic improvements to the investigation are stated.

strands.name: Methodological weaknesses
strands.content: The report describes specific methodological weaknesses or limitations.

strands.name: Improvements
strands.content: Realistic improvements to the investigation that are relevant to the identified weaknesses or limitations, are described.

strands.name: Methodological weaknesses
strands.content: The report explains the relative impact of specific methodological weaknesses or limitations.

strands.name: Improvements
strands.content: Realistic improvements to the investigation, that are relevant to the identified weaknesses or limitations, are explained.
